title: Local Impurity Induced Growth and Scrambling in Clean Free Fermions
authors: Qucheng Gao, Vikram Ravindranath, Xiao Chen
abstract: We study impurity-induced particle growth and scrambling in clean one-dimensional free-fermion systems. We show that a single local impurity can act as a branching source: particle or operator weight propagates coherently into the free bulk, returns to the impurity, and is locally converted into additional degrees of freedom. We develop this branching picture in three complementary settings: a monitored free-fermion model with feedback, a fully unitary interacting particle model, and Heisenberg operator dynamics with an interacting impurity. In the monitored model, we find a feedback-driven transition for both boundary and bulk impurities. In the unitary particle and operator models, a boundary impurity gives rise to an analogous transition from saturation to sustained growth and scrambling. These results reveal how a single impurity can generate complex many-body dynamics in an otherwise clean and free quantum system.
summary: This paper demonstrates that a single local impurity can act as a branching source, driving a transition from particle saturation to sustained growth and operator scrambling in free-fermion chains. The dynamics are governed by the return probability of particles to the impurity, which varies depending on whether the impurity is in the bulk or at the boundary. The findings reveal how local interactions can generate complex many-body behavior in otherwise simple, integrable systems.
main_problem: Investigating whether a single local impurity can induce nontrivial dynamics, particle growth, and information scrambling in an otherwise clean, simple free-fermion system.
main_result: The study identifies a transition from a saturating regime to a sustained growth and scrambling regime, driven by the interplay between local impurity branching and the return probability of the bulk.
method: The authors use a 'branching picture' analytical framework, large-scale MPS simulations using the ITensor library, and Laplace transform analysis of the single-particle propagator.
model_or_system: One-dimensional clean free-fermion systems, including monitored models with measurement-and-feedback, unitary interacting particle models, and Heisenberg operator dynamics with local impurities.
key_observables: Total particle number N(t), impurity occupation N_imp(t), particle-production current J(t), impurity-sensitive operator weight w(t), and operator entanglement entropy.
important_parameters: Monitoring probability p_m, impurity coupling strength Delta, impurity location (boundary vs. bulk), and the power-law decay exponent alpha of the return kernel.
main_assumptions: The branching picture is a heuristic analogy rather than a literal description of coherent quantum dynamics; the derivation relies on the spectrum being bounded and dispersion being quadratic.
figures_summary: Schematic of the monitored model protocol; plots of total particle number and impurity occupation showing saturation vs. linear growth; and plots of operator weight decay and entropy scaling.
paper_structure: Introduction of the impurity-induced growth problem, presentation of the monitored model and branching mechanism, analysis of unitary particle and operator models, investigation of scaling laws and dimensionality, and discussion of parity-breaking and topological effects.
why_interesting: It provides fundamental insights into how local perturbations can trigger many-body complexity and non-equilibrium phase transitions in simple systems, which is highly relevant to understanding non-equilibrium dynamics and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Monitored model with feedback. (a) Schematic illustration of the protocol. At each time step, the system first evolves unitarily for a time interval δt = 0.5, after which measurements are performed at impurity sites with proba- bility pm. (b),(c) Boundary impurity: total particle number N(t) and impurity occupation Nimp(t). (d),(e) Bulk impurity: total particle number N(t) and impurity occupation Nimp(t). In (c) and (e), the main panels are shown on log-log scales for weak monitoring, while the insets show large pm on linear scales. The black solid lines indicate the power-law decay t−3. Here, L = 1000, and we average over 200 samples.

FIG. 2. Interacting particle model. (a),(b) Boundary im- purity: total particle number N(t) and impurity occupation Nimp(t). (c),(d) Bulk impurity: total particle number N(t) and impurity occupation Nimp(t). Results are shown for L = 400. In (b) and (d), the black solid lines indicate the power-law decays t−3 and t−1, respectively. The inset in (c) shows the small-∆data on a linear-log scale, highlighting the continued slow growth of N(t) over the accessible time win- dow.

FIG. 3. Impurity-sensitive operator weight w(t) in the op- erator model with H = H0 + ∆NiNi+1. (a) Boundary im- purity, with i = 1 and O(0) = N1, for ∆= 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 and L = 400. (b) Bulk impurity, with i = L/2 and O(0) = NL/2, for ∆= 0.05, 0.10, 0.15 and L = 200. Solid and dashed lines indicate the power-law decays w(t) ∼t−3 in (a) and w(t) ∼t−1 in (b).

FIG. 4. Half-system operator entanglement entropy in the operator model. (a) Boundary impurity. (b) Bulk impurity. For small ∆, the entropy grows slowly. Inset: L = 200 data on a linear-log scale, highlighting the slow late-time growth. From light to dark colors: L = 50, 100, 150, 200. The late- time upturn in (a) near t/L ≃0.75 is a finite-size reflection effect from the opposite boundary.
